coredata.dc:title: The synthesis and efficiency investigation of a boronic acid-modified magnetic chitosan quantum dot nanocomposite in the detection of Cu2+ ions
coredata.dc:creator: Rafiee, Fatemeh
Tajfar, Niloofar
Mohammadnejad, Masoumeh
coredata.dc:description: We prepared the magnetic chitosan carbon quantum dot nanoparticles (Fe3O4@CQD NPs) via the hydrothermal treatment of chitosan biopolymer and then its magnetization with Fe3O4 nanoparticles. (4-Acetylphenyl)boronic acid compound was utilized for the modification of surface of Fe3O4@CQD nanoparticles via the covalent imine bond formation between NH2 groups of chitosan quantum dot with carbonyl functional of acetyl-substituted arylboronic acid. The synthesized Fe3O4@CQD@AP-B(OH)2 was characterized by FE-SEM, EDS, XRD, VSM and ICP-OES analysis and its fluorescence property was studied. This magnetic multifunctional nanoplatform sensor has shown high potential sensitivity for Cu2+ ions (in the range of 1.0–30.0 μM with limit of detection 0.3 μM) through interaction of cupric ions with the boronic-acid moiety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chitosan carbon quantum dot
Boronic acid-functionalized chitosan
Copper ions sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of carbon quantum dot from chitosan biopolymer
Functionalization of Fe3O4@CQD NPs with (4-acetylphenyl)boronic acid
The synthesis and characterization of Fe3O4@CQD@AP-B(OH)2 nanocomposite
The study of the efficiency of Fe3O4@CQD@AP-B(OH)2 nanocomposite in the determination of Cu2+

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank the partial financial support received from the research council of Alzahra University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FE-SEM and TEM images of Fe3O4@CQD@AP-B(OH)2 nanocomposite.
The FT-IR spectrum of Chitosan, CQD, Fe3O4 NPs, Fe3O4@CQD and Fe3O4@CQD@AP-B(OH)2.
The comparison of the fluorescence intensity of Fe3O4@CQD and Fe3O4@CQD@AP-B(OH)2.
The fluorescence quenching of Fe3O4@CQD@AP-B(OH)2 in the presence of different concentration of Cu2+ ions, insert: Calibration curve.
The fluorescence change of sensor after addition of different cation ions.
The comparison of the fluorescence quenching of Fe3O4@CQD and Fe3O4@CQD@AP-B(OH) sensors.
The preparation of Fe3O4@Chitosan carbon quantum dots.
The functionalization of Fe3O4@CQD with (4-acetylphenyl)boronic acid.
The interaction of Fe3O4@CQD@AP-B(OH)2 nanocomposite with copper ions.

head.ce:title: The synthesis and efficiency investigation of a boronic acid-modified magnetic chitosan quantum dot nanocomposite in the detection of Cu2+ ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Physics and Chemistry, Alzahra University, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Physics and Chemistry, Alzahra University, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We prepared the magnetic chitosan carbon quantum dot nanoparticles (Fe3O4@CQD NPs) via the hydrothermal treatment of chitosan biopolymer and then its magnetization with Fe3O4 nanoparticles. (4-Acetylphenyl)boronic acid compound was utilized for the modification of surface of Fe3O4@CQD nanoparticles via the covalent imine bond formation between NH2 groups of chitosan quantum dot with carbonyl functional of acetyl-substituted arylboronic acid. The synthesized Fe3O4@CQD@AP-B(OH)2 was characterized by FE-SEM, EDS, XRD, VSM and ICP-OES analysis and its fluorescence property was studied. This magnetic multifunctional nanoplatform sensor has shown high potential sensitivity for Cu2+ ions (in the range of 1.0–30.0 μM with limit of detection 0.3 μM) through interaction of cupric ions with the boronic-acid moiety.

ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of carbon quantum dots from available, biodegradable and biocompatible natural polysaccharides as starting carbon source has received tremendous attention. Chitosan is the N-deacetylated derivative of chitin, a renewable natural polysaccharide derived from crab and shrimp, having numerous NH2 and OH functional groups which makes it an interesting source for carbon quantum dots synthesis [1]. The hydrothermal carbonization of chitosan was utilized as a convenient, green, low-cost and mild synthetic method for the preparation of the amino-functionalized fluorescent carbon nanoparticles even in large-scale production [2–3]. The low toxicity, variable photoluminescence property, bioconjugation, high chemical and photostability of chitosan based carbon quantum dots have emerged them as extremely promising candidate in drug delivery, bioimaging, biolabeling, fluorescent chemo and biosensor and environmental pollutant detectors [4–8].

The composition and existing functional groups on the carbon dots surface affect the optical performance. Recently, a large number of researches have been focused on the surface modification methods of carbon quantum dots to improve the fluorescence quantum yields, complexation capacity and quenching capability. The surface hydroxyl, carboxyl and amine groups of chitosan carbon dot could have acted as tethering functional for further modifications such as amidation, esterification, sulfonylation and etc. [9]. Pawar and co-workers modified a chitosan based carbon dot with a nitrogen-containing heterocyclic compound and used it as a nanoprobe for the rapid detection of trace amount of water in organic solvents [10]. Janus et al. prepared the amino acid functionalized chitosan carbon quantum dots and suggested that they be promising in biomedical application [11]. Song et al. used chitosan biopolymer as carbon source for the quantum dot synthesis that acted also as reducing agent and stabilizer for Au nanoparticle preparation. They applied the CQDs/Au nanocomposite for I− ion detection by the colorimetric method [12]. Zhang et al. designed a pH-sensitive N-doped carbon dots–heparin drug delivery system for doxorubicin release by using chitosan as carbon source and its functionalization with heparin via amide bond formation [13].

Owing to the crucial role of Cu2+ ion in electron transfer biological processes and its bioaccumulative properties that in its amounts excessive led to risk in plants and animals, a number of studies have focused on quantum dots applications in this field [14]. Lia et al. developed a fluorescent probe for Cu2+ detection based on the decrease of relative fluorescence intensity of CdS quantum dot modified by chitosan after adding cupric ions [15]. Xiong and co-workers prepared a 3-aminophenylboronic acid-functionalized CdTe quantum dot probe for the determination of copper ions in spiked human serum and water samples [16]. In this line, the use of magnetic fluorescence nanoparticles promises to expand their application in diverse fields due to easy magnetically separation. Kumar and co-workers developed the synthesis of fluorescent magnetic chitosan nanoparticles for Cu2+ ion sensing and also cell imaging [17]. Xie e al. synthesized the magnetic fluorescent CS–Fe3O4@CdSeS nanoparticles for simultaneous removal and optical determination of trace copper ions [18].

FT-IR spectra were obtained by using a Bruker Tensor 27 spectrometer within the 400–4000 cm−1 range. Quantitative inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) measurements were collected using a 730-ES Varian instrument. Magnetic susceptibility measurements were performed at room temperature using a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM, LBKFB model, Iran) with the magnetic field range of between +10,000 and −10,000 Oe. XRD pattern of the catalyst was obtained by an X-ray diffractometer (Ultima model, Rigaku company) using monochromatic Cu-Ka radiation at room temperature. FE-SEM instrument (TESCAN, MIRA3 model) was used to study the morphology of the catalyst. EDX analysis was recorded using a Mapping and Line Scan instrument (with oxford detector, England). Thin-layer chromatography (TLC) with commercial aluminum-backed plates of silica gel 60 F254 under UV light and Agilent 6890 GC and GC–MS model Agilent Series 5973 were used for tracking the progression of the catalytic reactions. Ultrasonic device (BANDELIN electronic model) with power of 160/640 W and frequency 3 was used for dispersion of nanoparticles. All materials were bought from Merck, Alfa-Aeser Sigma Aldrich, and Exir and used as received without further purification. Chitosan (from crab shells, powder form, molecular weight: 100,000-300,000, deacetylated degree: ≥85 and viscosity dynamical: 20–200 cP, 1% in 1% acetic acid) was bought from EXIR GmbH (EX-012528.0025).

Magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles were synthesized according to the literature data through a conventional co-precipitation process from commercial salts contain FeCl3·6H2O and FeCl2·4H2O in 2:1 ratio [19].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon quantum dot from chitosan biopolymer
ce:section.ce:para: The amount of 2 g chitosan was added to 18 mL 2% acetic acid solution and stirred at room temperature until homogeneous. Then, the mixture sealed into a Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave and placed under hydrothermal treatment conditions in a furnace at 180 °C for 12 h. After this time, autoclave was cooled down to ambient temperature and obtained dark brown solution was centrifuged at a speed of 800 rpm for 20 min to remove the less-fluorescent deposit. The upper brown solution was stored in the refrigerator for the later uses [2].

Magnetic chitosan carbon quantum dot nanoparticles were prepared through the treatment of 0.5 g of Fe3O4 nanoparticles with 3 mL of carbon quantum dot solution in 10 mL water under ultra-sonication conditions for 30 min and then stirring at 60 °C for 3 h. The Fe3O4@CQD nanoparticles were separated magnetically, washed with water and finally dried at ambient temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functionalization of Fe3O4@CQD NPs with (4-acetylphenyl)boronic acid
ce:section.ce:para: 0.5 g of Fe3O4@CQD nanoparticles and 0.1 g of (4-acetylphenyl)boronic acid was mixed in 3 mL ethanol and stirred for 24 h under reflux conditions. The obtained Fe3O4@CQD@AP-B(OH)2 nanocomposite was separated magnetically, washed with ethanol and water and dried at room temperature.

To study the fluorescence properties of synthetic nanocomposite, 2 mL of dispersed solution of Fe3O4@CQD@AP-B(OH)2 were transferred to spectrofluorimetric cell and emission spectra were recorded in different excitation wavelength (350–430 nm). The Fe3O4@CQD@AP-B(OH)2 exhibited major fluorescence peak at 540 nm in excitation wavelength 390 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synthesis and characterization of Fe3O4@CQD@AP-B(OH)2 nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: Chitosan biopolymer was used as a green carbon rich source for the synthesis of carbon based quantum dots. The treatment of chitosan based carbon dots with Fe3O4 nanoparticles led to formation of a magnetic support (Scheme 1 ). Carbonization of chitosan increases the number of functional groups on the nanocomposite surface and this improves the possibility of further functionalization.

In the next step, the prepared magnetic carbon quantum dot nanoparticles were modified with (4-acetylphenyl)boronic acid through Schiff base formation between NH2 groups of chitosan quantum dots and carbonyl functional of phenyl boronic acid derivative (Scheme 2 ).

After than the synthesis and characterization of Fe3O4@CQD@AP-B(OH)2 nanocomposite, its performance was examined in the absorption of Cu2+ ions in solution via the cupric ions binding to hydroxyl functional of boronic acid segment (Scheme 3 ).
The Fe3O4@CQD@AP-B(OH)2 nanocomposite was characterized by FE-SEM, EDS, XRD, VSM and ICP-OES analysis.

The field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) and TEM images in different magnifications was studied to specify the morphology and nanoparticles size of Fe3O4@CQD@AP-B(OH)2 nanocomposite. As seen in Fig. 1 , the nanoparticles are spherical with average size of ~10 nm in diameter.

In the energy dispersion spectrometry (EDS) (Fig. 2 ), the presence of Fe and O signals as the components of the magnetic core and also C, N, O and B signals as the elemental composition of carbon based quantum dot and 4-acetophenyl boronic acid emphasizes the formation of Fe3O4@CQD@AP-B(OH)2 nanocomposite. The exact amount of B as determined by inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES) was 0.11 mmol/g.

The main diffraction peaks in the powder XRD pattern of the Fe3O4@CQD@AP-B(OH)2 nanocomposite was appeared at 2θ = 30.17, 35.46, 43.38, 53.69, 57.23 and 62.77° corresponding to the crystalline planes (220), (311), (400), (422), (511) and (440), respectively that are in accordance with the XRD pattern of Fe3O4 nanoparticles with cubic spinel structure (Fig. 3 ).

The saturation magnetization value of the Fe3O4@CQD@AP-B(OH)2 nanocomposite was measured by using of a vibrating sample magnetometer at room temperature (Fig. 4 ) and obtained 39 emu g−1 that is lower than the bare Fe3O4 because of the non-magnetic components on the surface of magnetic core.

The FT-IR spectrum of chitosan, CQD, Fe3O4 NPs, Fe3O4@CQD and Fe3O4@CQD@AP-B(OH)2 are shown in Fig. 5 . The OH and NH stretching vibrations of chitosan are observed at 3472 cm−1. The peaks at 2920–2875 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of CH bonds. The vibrations of CO (acetamide functional) are appeared at 1660 cm−1. The peak at 1596 cm−1 is assigned to the bending vibration of primary NH bond. Also, the bands at 1094 and 895 cm−1 are related to the skeletal vibrations of glycosidic structure of chitosan and pyranose rings. In the FT-IR analysis of CQD, the presence of amine, hydroxyl and carbonyl functionality are detected while the intensity of OH and NH stretching vibrations is decreased in compared with pure chitosan, the glycosidic and pyranose ring adsorption bands are disappeared due to degradation of chitosan chains and decomposition of pyranose ring and new bands related to aromatic structure are observed at 1522 cm−1 [1,11,20]. In FT-IR spectra of Fe3O4 nanoparticles, the strong band at 582 and broad band at 3380 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration FeO and OH bonds, respectively. In the spectrum of Fe3O4@CQD@AP-B(OH)2 nanocomposite, the covalent bonding of 4-acetylphenylboronic acid on the magnetic chitosan based carbon quantum dot is confirmed by the presence of peaks at 1560, 1515, 830 and 630 cm−1 according to the bonds of p-di-substituted benzene [21]. The peak intensity at 1596 cm−1 is decreased due to imine bond formation and the characteristic absorption band of BO bond seemed at (1333, 1340, 1381) cm−1 [22–24].

The comparison of fluorescent intensity of Fe3O4@CQD and Fe3O4@CQD@AP-B(OH)2 are given in Fig. 6 . The fluorescence quenching of functionalized Fe3O4@CQD was observed due to electron donating of amine groups of Fe3O4@CQD to carbonyl functional of (4-acetylphenyl)boronic acid and also partial agglomeration of quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The study of the efficiency of Fe3O4@CQD@AP-B(OH)2 nanocomposite in the determination of Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: 0.003 g of Fe3O4@CQD@AP-B(OH)2 was added to 100 mL distillated water and dispersed under ultrasonic conditions for 15 min. Then, nanocomposite was separated magnetically and washed with water and this procedure containing dispersion and washing repeated for 8 times. The photoluminescence intensity of the synthesized compound varied with the variation of excitation wavelength from 350 to 430 nm and the maximum emission spectrum was resulted at 390 nm. Finally, to 2 mL of dispersed solution was added distillated water (2 mL) and the fluorescence emission spectra was recorded under excitation at 390 nm.

To investigation of the interaction between cupric ions and nanocomposite, different concentrations of CuCl2 solution (1, 3, 5, 10, 20 and 30 μM) were added to 2 mL of dispersed solution of Fe3O4@CQD@AP-B(OH)2 and stirred for 30 min. Then, the fluorescent intensity of dispersed solution of Fe3O4@CQD@AP-B(OH)2 was recorded in the presence of various concentrations of CuCl2 solution. As shown in Fig. 7 , the photoluminescence intensity of Fe3O4@CQD@AP-B(OH)2 was quenched by increasing the analyte solution (different concentration of CuCl2 solution). There is a linear relationship between fluorescence decreasing (ΔF) of Fe3O4@CQD@AP-B(OH)2 nanocomposite with concentration of Cu2+. The results show the synthetic nanocomposite could be used as a sensor for determination of cupric ion in the range of 1.0–30.0 μM with limit of detection 0.3 μM.

To evaluate the effect of other cation ions on the fluorescence intensity of synthetic fluorescence sensor, solution of Ni2+, Mg2+, Zn2+ and Pb2+ were studied by the same procedure for Cu2+ (Fig. 8 ). The results exhibited that the fluorescence intensity of the sensor was affected by these common metal ions less than Cu2+.

The high binding affinity of Cu2+ ions to surface hydroxyl groups of boronic acid functionalized magnetic CQD sensor and complex formation through electron pair sharing led to reduce of the fluorescent intensity of Fe3O4@CQD@AP-B(OH)2. The functional groups of unmodified chitosan carbon dot contain amine, hydroxyl and carboxyl especially amine groups also could donate their electron to copper ions. To show the valuable effect of boric acid groups on the surface carbon quantum dots, we examined the fluorescence quenching of Fe3O4@CQD as sensor in the presence of 10 μm Cu2+ solution and compared with Fe3O4@CQD@AP-B(OH)2 at the same conditions (Fig. 9 ). The results show well that the presence of boronic acid functional increases the sensitivity to copper.

The magnetic carbon quantum dot nanoparticles were prepared by using of chitosan as a green carbon and nitrogen rich source and Fe3O4 nanoparticles. Then, (4-acetylphenyl)boronic acid functionalized magnetic CQD were synthesized and utilized as an efficient sensor for Cu2+ ions. The fluorescence quenching of unmodified magnetic CQD and boronic acid-functionalized magnetic CQD were examined in the equal concentration of Cu2+ solution. Due to the binding affinity of Cu2+ ions to hydroxyl groups of boronic acid, the fluorescent intensity of modified nanocomposite more decreases. Also, the prepared sensor showed more sensitivity to copper ions than some metal cations (M2+, M = Mg, Ni, Zn and Pb).

Fatemeh Rafiee: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Resources, Writing - Original Draft, Writing - Review & Editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.
Niloofar Tajfar: Software, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data Curation, Visualization.
Masoumeh Mohammadnejad: Expositor of analytical analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the partial financial support received from the research council of Alzahra University.
